persons.name: Deepak
persons.context: Deepak was found unconscious with severe head injuries near Nehru Nagar, Anand Parbat, Delhi. He was taken to LHMC Hospital but later succumbed to his injuries, with the cause of death determined as blunt force trauma to the head.

persons.name: Ravinder
persons.context: Ravinder is the accused in the case, allegedly involved in an assault on the victim during a quarrel at a marriage function. He was interrogated by police, and charges were filed against him under Section 302 IPC (Murder) after the victim died.

persons.name: Dinesh Sharma
persons.context: Dinesh Sharma, the victim’s brother, provided a witness statement indicating he saw the accused near the scene of the incident. He is identified as an eyewitness to the events leading to the injury.

title: State Vs Ravindraaa
caseText:
CASE FILE: State vs Ravinder
Court: Sessions Court, Delhi
FIR No: 525/2016
Police Station: Anand Parbat, Delhi
Date of FIR: 04 December 2016
INCIDENT SUMMARY:
Victim Deepak was found unconscious with severe head injuries near Nehru Nagar, Anand Parbat, Delhi. A PCR call led police to the scene, and the victim was taken to LHMC Hospital. Initial investigation suggests a quarrel between the accused Ravinder and the victim at a marriage function that escalated into physical assault.
ACCUSED:
Ravinder – allegedly involved in assault leading to fatal injuries
IPC Sections Alleged: 307 IPC (Attempt to Murder), later upgraded to 302 IPC (Murder) after victim succumbed
EVIDENCE COLLECTED BY POLICE:
Witness statement of victim’s brother (saw accused near the scene).
Postmortem examination in hospital (cause: blunt force trauma to head).
Clothes seized from accused with blood stains (sent to FSL).
Accused interrogation notes.
Timeline of incident and initial hospital report.
WITNESSES:
Dinesh Sharma (victim’s brother) – eyewitness to events leading to injury
Hospital staff at LHMC – treated victim on arrival
Police officers present at initial scene
CHARGESHEET / COURT ENTRY:
Investigating Officer filed chargesheet under Section 302 IPC (Murder)
Court received the charge sheet and summoned accused Ravinder
Accused pleaded not guilty

transcript.speaker: Court
transcript.content: Court is now in session. The Honorable Judge presiding. This is the matter of: State Vs Ravindraaa. All parties are present. Proceedings will begin with Pre-Trial Motions.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content: The court is now in session. This matter concerns the State versus Ravinder, FIR No. 525/2016 registered at Anand Parbat Police Station, wherein the accused stands charged under Section 302 IPC for the alleged murder of Deepak. The prosecution has filed a chargesheet, and the accused has pleaded not guilty—proceedings shall now commence with pre-trial motions. Counsel may raise any preliminary applications at this time.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State respectfully submits that the chargesheet filed under Section 302 IPC is fully supported by credible evidence, including the eyewitness account of the victim’s brother, forensic reports confirming blunt force trauma as the cause of death, and blood-stained clothing recovered from the accused. We oppose any application for discharge or bail at this stage, as the prima facie case against the accused is strong and the offense carries the gravest consequences under law. The prosecution is ready to proceed with framing of charges and presentation of evidence without delay.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, while the prosecution asserts a strong prima facie case, we submit that the evidence is circumstantial and riddled with gaps—there is no direct proof linking the accused to the fatal assault, and the so-called eyewitness account lacks corroboration and consistency. Furthermore, the recovery of blood-stained clothes raises more questions than answers, particularly regarding chain of custody and the circumstances of seizure, which we intend to challenge under Section 23 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam. In light of these material doubts and the absence of conclusive forensic linkage, we respectfully urge the court to consider our application for discharge under Section 230 of the BNSS, as the prosecution has failed to establish a case that would warrant framing of charges.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has carefully considered the submissions of both the prosecution and the defense at this pre-trial stage. While the defense urges discharge under Section 230 of the BNSS, the court finds that the chargesheet, supported by the eyewitness account of Dinesh Sharma, the postmortem report indicating death due to blunt force trauma, and the recovery of blood-stained clothing from the accused, discloses sufficient grounds to proceed further.  

**Order!** The application for discharge is hereby **rejected**. The court is satisfied that a prima facie case exists to frame charges under Section 302 IPC. The prosecution may proceed with the framing of charges.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State firmly maintains that the chargesheet under Section 302 IPC is not only legally sound but substantiated by credible and cogent evidence—starting with the eyewitness testimony of Dinesh Sharma, who placed the accused at the scene moments before the assault, corroborated by the postmortem report confirming death due to blunt force trauma consistent with a violent attack, and further strengthened by the recovery of blood-stained clothing from the accused, which has been sent to the FSL for forensic analysis. We submit that the chain of events, as revealed in the investigation, leaves no reasonable doubt that the accused, Ravinder, committed the fatal assault following a quarrel at the marriage function, and we are prepared to prove his guilt beyond reasonable doubt at trial. The prosecution respectfully requests that the court proceed with the framing of charges so that justice may be served for the deceased and his grieving family.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, while the prosecution relies heavily on the testimony of Dinesh Sharma, we submit that his account is uncorroborated, inconsistent with the timeline, and fails to establish beyond reasonable doubt that the accused delivered the fatal blow. The blood-stained clothes allegedly recovered from the accused lack a clear chain of custody, and under Section 23 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, any evidence obtained without strict procedural compliance is inherently suspect. Given these critical gaps and the absence of direct forensic linkage, we maintain that the charges under Section 302 IPC cannot stand and urge the court to reconsider the framing of charges in light of these material deficiencies.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has heard the arguments from both sides regarding the framing of charges under Section 302 IPC. While the defense raises concerns about the chain of custody and the reliability of eyewitness testimony, the law requires only a prima facie satisfaction at this stage—not proof beyond reasonable doubt.  

**Order!** Charges are hereby framed against the accused, Ravinder, under Section 302 IPC for the murder of Deepak. The accused shall be asked to enter a plea.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, now that charges have been framed under Section 302 IPC, the prosecution stands ready to present a clear and compelling case grounded in credible evidence: the eyewitness account of Dinesh Sharma, who saw the accused assault the victim following a heated quarrel; the postmortem report confirming death by blunt force trauma consistent with that assault; and the recovery of blood-stained clothing from the accused, directly linking him to the crime. We will demonstrate that the accused acted with the intention to cause death, and we trust the court will find the evidence sufficient to sustain a conviction.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, while the prosecution seeks to frame this as a clear-cut case of murder, we submit that the evidence is far from conclusive—there is no direct proof that the accused struck the fatal blow, and the so-called eyewitness account is uncorroborated and riddled with inconsistencies that undermine its credibility. Furthermore, the recovery of blood-stained clothing lacks a documented chain of custody, raising serious questions under Section 23 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, and without reliable forensic linkage, the prosecution’s theory rests on speculation, not proof. At this critical stage, where the burden is on the State to establish a prima facie case worthy of trial, we urge the court to recognize that reasonable doubt permeates every aspect of the prosecution’s narrative.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has carefully reviewed the chargesheet, the evidence on record, and the arguments presented by both the prosecution and the defense. While the defense raises valid concerns regarding the chain of custody and the reliability of eyewitness testimony, the law at the stage of charge framing requires only that the material before the court, if unrebutted, would be sufficient to establish the offense.  

**Order!** The accused, Ravinder, pleads not guilty to the charge under Section 302 IPC. The trial shall now proceed to the next phase—Opening Statements. The prosecution may begin.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State will prove beyond reasonable doubt that the accused, Ravinder, brutally assaulted the deceased, Deepak, following a heated quarrel at a marriage function, delivering fatal blows that caused blunt force trauma to the head—confirmed by the postmortem report—and fled the scene, leaving the victim unconscious near Nehru Nagar. The eyewitness account of Dinesh Sharma, the victim’s brother, places the accused at the scene moments before the assault, and the recovery of blood-stained clothing from the accused directly links him to this heinous crime. We will demonstrate that the accused acted with clear intention to cause death, and the evidence, when viewed as a whole, leaves no room for reasonable doubt.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the prosecution’s case rests on conjecture, not certainty—there is no direct evidence that the accused struck the fatal blow, and the so-called eyewitness account of Dinesh Sharma is uncorroborated, inconsistent, and fails to eliminate the very real possibility that a third party caused the victim’s injuries. The recovery of blood-stained clothing lacks a documented chain of custody, violating procedural safeguards under Section 23 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, and without reliable forensic confirmation linking those stains to the deceased, the prosecution’s theory collapses into speculation. At every turn, reasonable doubt permeates the State’s narrative, and it is our solemn duty to ensure that no conviction is secured on the basis of suspicion alone.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has heard the opening statements from both the prosecution and the defense. The prosecution alleges that the accused, Ravinder, committed murder under Section 302 IPC following a quarrel at a marriage function, while the defense contends that the evidence is circumstantial, lacks corroboration, and fails to establish guilt beyond reasonable doubt.  

**Order!** The trial shall now proceed to the examination of prosecution witnesses. The prosecution may call its first witness to the stand.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, the State calls Dinesh Sharma, the brother of the deceased, to the stand—a witness who will testify that he saw the accused, Ravinder, in a heated altercation with his brother Deepak moments before the fatal assault, and who observed the accused strike Deepak with a blunt object near Nehru Nagar, after which the victim collapsed unconscious. This eyewitness account, consistent with the postmortem report confirming death due to blunt force trauma, forms the cornerstone of our case, and we will demonstrate that the accused acted with clear intention to cause death.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Your Honor, while the prosecution presents Dinesh Sharma as a reliable eyewitness, we will demonstrate that his testimony is fraught with inconsistencies, lacks independent corroboration, and fails to conclusively place the accused at the precise moment of the fatal assault—creating more doubt than certainty. Moreover, the absence of any forensic confirmation directly linking the accused to the weapon or the specific injuries suffered by the deceased renders this entire narrative speculative. Under Section 116 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, the benefit of every reasonable doubt must go to the accused, and here, those doubts are not only present—they are overwhelming.
